raw_case:
Urothelial carcinoma of the renal pelvis with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation complicated by hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis: a case report and literature review


Background

Renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with multiple histological variants co-occurs with hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis extremely rarely. The diagnosis of renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma can be complicated by these conditions, leading to delayed or missed diagnosis or misdiagnosis.

Case presentation

We report the evolutionary course of a rare case of renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma containing squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation associated with hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis in a low-functioning kidney. Preoperative clinical and imaging manifestations were highly suggestive of hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis; however, all corresponding therapeutic measures failed. Eventually, nephrectomy was performed, and postoperative pathological examination revealed renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation. Further residual ureter and bladder cuff resection was refused by the patient. Regular cisplatin-based postoperative chemotherapy and bladder perfusion were arranged, and cystoscopy and imaging examinations were performed regularly for follow-up.

Conclusion

Hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis can mask the typical clinical and imaging manifestations of renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma. Renal pelvic malignancy should be considered in patients with intractable pyelonephritis and hydronephrosis. There is no standard treatment for renal pelvic carcinoma with multiple pathological types. Radical surgery is considered an appropriate option and should be performed as early as possible. Chemotherapy and immunotherapy may improve patient survival.

Background

Renal pelvis and ureter urothelial carcinomas (UCs) are considered rare tumours, accounting for only 5–10% of all UCs [1]. Among them, renal pelvic UCs with histological variants are extremely rare. A retrospective study of 115 cases of upper urinary tract urothelial carcinoma (UUTUC) showed that 7% of cases were associated with squamous differentiation, 6% were associated with sarcomatoid differentiation, and 4% were associated with glandular differentiation [2]. In this report, we present a case involving a patient diagnosed with renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with two histological variants: squamous and sarcomatoid. The patient underwent flexible ureteroscopic lithotripsy one year earlier, which did not reveal any tumours in the renal pelvis or calyces. Furthermore, the patient also presented with hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis, which mask the typical clinical and imaging manifestations of UUTUCs, resulting in difficulty in diagnosis and leading to delayed diagnosis of renal pelvic UC. The presence of multiple histological variants, the associated hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis, and the fact that the tumour occurred within one year after flexible ureteroscopy, make this case extremely rare. At present, no similar cases have been reported in studies published at home or abroad. We report this rare case to share our experience and serve as a reference for achieving a better understanding of similar cases and avoiding delayed or missed diagnosis and misdiagnosis of UC.

Case presentation

A 56-year-old woman presented to the hospital with right waist distension. Computed tomography (CT) revealed severe hydronephrosis (Fig. 1A) caused by a single ureteral stone (Fig. 1B). Renal nuclear scintigraphy showed that the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) of the right kidney was 20 ml/min. Percutaneous renal puncture drainage was performed, and the waist distension subsequently disappeared. Two months after drainage, repeat CT showed improvement in the hydronephrosis (Fig. 1C, D), and flexible ureteroscopic lithotripsy was performed. No tumours or any suspected lesions were found in the pelvis or calyces of the kidney under flexible ureteroscopy. Stones in the right ureter and lower calyx were both removed. Due to a satisfactory postoperative recovery, the patient did not return to the hospital for follow-up for one year.Thirteen months after the surgery, the patient was admitted again with pain in the right waist combined with intermittent moderate fever for the prior month. Physical examination showed positive percussion pain in the right renal region. CT showed an enlarged right kidney (Fig. 1E). Hydronephrosis with internal segregation was also seen on enhanced CT (Fig. 1F). Urine and blood cultures were negative. Routine urine tests revealed many pus cells per high-power field. Routine blood tests showed that the total leucocyte count was mildly increased.Her initial diagnosis was pyelonephritis; the hydronephrosis was likely the result of severe mucosal inflammation. Empirical intravenous antimicrobial therapy was given for 1 week, but no symptom relief was observed. Due to obvious internal separation caused by the hydronephrosis, a ureteral stent was placed for drainage of the dilated upper calyx, and percutaneous puncture was performed to place 2 tubes for drainage of the dilated middle and lower calices. A total of 50–150 ml of cloudy purulent urine was drained daily through the percutaneous puncture tubes. The patient’s temperature returned to normal 3 days after drainage, and the pain gradually resolved.One month after drainage, the patient had no other discomfort except for occasional mild pain in the right lumbar region. CT showed that the hydronephrosis had resolved, and no signs of pyelonephritis were found (Fig. 1G).Three months after drainage, the right ureteral stent was removed, but the right renal puncture tubes were retained. Surprisingly, 2 weeks after stent removal, intermittent fever developed again, accompanied by worsened pain in the right waist. Abdominal CT showed hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis (Fig. 1H, I). Empirical intravenous antimicrobial therapy was administered for 1 week but without symptom improvement.
Considering the intractable infection and hydronephrosis of the right kidney with abnormal morphology and low function, right nephrectomy was performed. Intraoperatively, the texture of the right kidney was similar to that of a sac. Except for the tight adhesion between the ureter and the surrounding tissues at the renal portal, there was no severe adhesion to the surrounding tissues in the remainder of the kidney. The right kidney and ureter above the iliac vessel were completely removed. Atrophy of the renal parenchyma, which was polycystic, and erosion of the inferior renal calyx and surrounding tissues with purulent discharge and unclear boundaries were observed in the gross specimen (Fig. 2A).Postoperative pathological examination revealed that the tumour was located in the lower calyces of the kidney where stones had been present. The prepared kidney section was inhomogenous, presenting with necrosis and abscess formation. The malignant urothelial cells had invaded the renal pelvis and parenchyma but not the renal peritoneum and perinephric fat. Tumour invasion was also observed in the renal nerves but not the blood vessels. Although the tumour margins were negative, cancer tissue was observed in the collagen fibrous tissue around the ureter. Immunohistochemistry revealed positivity for CKpan, vimentin, GATA-3, P40, P63, P53, and Ki-67 (Fig. 2C, D, E, F, G, H, I). Positive P63 is usually seen in healthy population, however, positivity for GATA-3, P53, P40 and a Ki-67 proliferation index of up to 70% indicated the malignant nature of the lesion. Furthermore, positive CKpan indicated the epithelial origin of the lesion, whereas positive vimentin indicated mesenchymal tissue component. Based on the histological and immunohistochemical findings, the final diagnosis was high-grade invasive renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with unusual histological variants, including squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation (Fig. 2B). The pathological stage was pT3NxMx according to the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) staging criteria.
The patient’s postoperative recovery was good, and her fever and right waist pain disappeared after the operation. She is currently undergoing regular intravenous cisplatin-based and intravesical perfusion chemotherapy. Cystoscopy and imaging examinations have been arranged regularly for the surveillance of tumour recurrence.

Discussion and conclusions

Concurrent squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation have rarely been reported in UC. According to a search of PubMed, the first case was reported in 2009 [3], and only eight cases have been reported to date [3–10]. Of them, seven cases occurred in the bladder or ureter, and only one case occurred in the renal pelvis [8].The UUTUC we reported not only had two histological variants of squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation but also occurred in the renal pelvis, indicating an incredibly rare variant. Furthermore, the patient presented simultaneously with hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis, which, in addition to emphasizing the rarity of the case, masked the clinical and imaging manifestations of the renal pelvic carcinoma and increased the diagnostic and treatment complexity, thus leading to a delayed diagnosis. To the best of our knowledge, there are no similar cases reported in the literature at home or abroad.The preoperative diagnosis of UUTUC mainly depends on imaging, cytological examination, and endoscopic pathological biopsy for patients with typical symptoms of haematuria. CT urography (CTU) is the most accurate imaging modality for diagnosing UUTUC [11]. A meta-analysis of 13 studies including 1233 patients showed that for UC, the diagnostic sensitivity and specificity of CTU reached 92% and 95%, respectively [12]. Magnetic resonance urography (MRU) is less sensitive and specific in diagnosing and staging UUTUC than CTU [13]. The sensitivity of MRU is 75% in detecting tumours smaller than 2 cm and is only indicated for patients who are contraindicated for CTU [14]. Cytology is only used as an auxiliary diagnostic method due to its low diagnostic sensitivity for UUTUC [15]. If imaging and cytology examinations cannot definitively confirm or rule out a diagnosis of UUTUC, diagnostic ureteroscopy and biopsy can be considered [16]. However, performing diagnostic ureteroscopy before surgery or performing biopsy during diagnostic ureteroscopy have been identified as risk factors for intravesical recurrence [17]. The patient we reported in this case lacked the typical symptom of haematuria but presented with typical clinical manifestations and CT imaging features of hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis. This fact prompted us to believe that this was a simple case of an intractable infected kidney with abnormal morphology and low function, which could be cured by surgical resection when adequate treatment for hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis had no effects. We therefore did not attempt further approaches with MRU, cytology or ureteroscopy to assess the possibility of a renal pelvic tumour but instead chose to perform radical nephrectomy directly.UUTUC with squamous or sarcomatoid differentiation is an extremely rare malignancy with a high risk of recurrence and metastasis and a poor prognosis [18]. The reported 5-year disease-specific survival rate is 48.9%, and the median survival time is 59 months if the histological type of UUTUC contains squamous differentiation [19]. Compared with patients with pure UC, fewer patients with sarcomatoid differentiation survive for more than two years [20]. At present, there is no standard treatment for UUTUC with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation. For patients with suspected UUTUC, radical nephroureterectomy plus bladder cuff resection is recommended as gold standard treatment [21]. One study revealed that across all patients, lymphadenectomy did not affect cancer-specific survival but that the extent of lymphadenectomy significantly impacted patient survival for those with stage pT3 or higher [22]. The use of an lymph node dissection template and the degree of completeness can improve cancer-specific survival in patients with muscle-invasive disease and reduce the risk of local recurrence [23]. Therefore, template-based lymph node dissection is recommended for all patients who are scheduled for radical nephroureterectomy, as preoperative tumour staging is difficult [24]. In this case, as the preoperative clinical data highly suggested hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis of a low-functioning kidney and did not support the diagnosis of a malignant tumour of the renal pelvis, only nephrectomy was performed, and the patient refused further residual ureter and bladder cuff resection. She is currently under regular cisplatin-based postoperative chemotherapy and bladder perfusion. Cystoscopy and imaging examinations are performed regularly for follow-up.Recent research on treating UUTUC has focused mainly on neoadjuvant chemotherapy, postoperative chemotherapy, and immunotherapy. For high-grade UUTUC patients with preoperatively obtained pathological evidence, the prospective data from a phase II trial show that the use of preoperative neoadjuvant chemotherapy can achieve a 14% pathological complete remission rate [25]. Compared with surgery alone, neoadjuvant chemotherapy can reduce the recurrence rate and mortality without causing adverse effects on subsequent surgery [26] and improve progression-free and overall survival [27]. A meta-analysis showed that neoadjuvant chemotherapy resulted in a pathological partial response rate of 43% and a downstaging rate of 33% and improved overall survival and cancer-specific survival over radical nephroureterectomy alone [28]. Patients with invasive UUTUC who received gemcitabine-platinum combination chemotherapy within 90 days after radical surgery had significantly longer disease-free survival than those who underwent postoperative follow-up alone [29]. However, the efficacy of postoperative chemotherapy was strongly correlated with the variant histology; only patients with pure UUTUC showed a significant improvement in overall survival, whereas UUTUC patients with squamous differentiation and sarcomatoid metaplasia showed no improvement [30]. This suggests that the patient in this case may not be benefitting from the chemotherapy she is receiving. However, a study showed that nivolumab significantly improved the disease-free survival of patients with high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma after radical surgery to 20.8 months, while the survival in the placebo group was only 10.8 months [31]. Therefore, immunotherapy might be a valid choice for the postoperative treatment of the patient in this case.Renal pelvic carcinoma with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation extremely rarely co-occurs with hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis. In these cases, however, the patients tend to exhibit the clinical and imaging manifestations of hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis, prompting doctors to adopt treatment measures targeting those conditions, potentially leading to misdiagnosis and missed or delayed diagnosis. Malignancy is typically suspected only when therapeutic measures against hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis, such as drainage and antimicrobial treatments, have failed. A definitive diagnosis depends on preoperative or postoperative pathologic examination. This case indicates that the typical CT imaging manifestations of renal pelvic carcinoma can be masked by the infected mucosa of the renal pelvis caused by hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis. While mechanism underlying sarcomatoid differentiation in renal pelvic tumours is unclear, urolithiasis and hydronephrosis are often associated with squamous differentiation [32]. This case also serves as a reminder that renal pelvic malignancy should be considered in patients with intractable pyelonephritis and nonobstructing hydronephrosis, especially in those with a longstanding history of urolithiasis and severe hydronephrosis.Renal pelvic malignancy should be strongly considered in any patient presenting with intractable hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis in a low-functioning kidney, and careful diagnostic workup should be performed. Surgical intervention should be performed as early as possible, keeping in mind the possibility of renal pelvic malignancy during the procedure.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 56-year-old woman.
- **Chief Complaint:** Right waist distension, later evolving to right waist pain with intermittent fever.
- **History of Present Illness:** 
  - Initial presentation: Right waist distension with imaging suggestive of severe hydronephrosis caused by a ureteral stone. Percutaneous renal puncture drainage was performed. 
  - One year later: Right waist pain and intermittent fever for one month. CT showed an enlarged right kidney with hydronephrosis and internal separation.
  - Empirical antimicrobial treatment and drainage failed to resolve symptoms, leading to nephrectomy for a non-functioning kidney.
- **Past Medical History:** History of flexible ureteroscopic lithotripsy one year earlier for ureteral and calyceal stones, with no apparent tumours identified at the time. No noted follow-up for one year.
- **Personal History:** None provided.
- **Family History:** None provided.
- **Physical Examination:** Positive percussion pain in the right renal region; fever present initially.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Imaging: Severe hydronephrosis and signs of pyelonephritis, later with abnormal kidney morphology and persistent internal separation.
  - Blood/urine cultures: Negative; routine blood work showed mildly elevated leukocyte count; urine test showed pus cells.
  - Pathology: Post-nephrectomy showed high-grade invasive renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Step 1: Initial Presentation (Severe Hydronephrosis, Likely Infectious Origin)**
  - Acute obstructive pyelonephritis and hydronephrosis secondary to ureteral stone were considered. Percutaneous drainage, antimicrobial treatment, and later stone removal were appropriately performed.
  - Alternatives such as renal malignancy not initially suspected as imaging and ureteroscopy showed no evidence of tumors.

- **Step 2: Recurrence of Symptoms**
  - Hydronephrosis and persistent fevers despite aggressive antimicrobial therapy raised suspicion of intractable pyelonephritis or atypical pathology. Recurrent infection from abnormal renal anatomy vs. an underlying malignancy were both considered.
  - Imaging features and a history of low kidney function justified right nephrectomy without further advanced diagnostics.

- **Step 3: Post-Surgical Pathological Diagnosis**
  - Postoperative pathological evaluation confirmed high-grade invasive renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with unusual squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation. Immunohistochemical markers supported the malignant epithelial and mesenchymal origin.

- **Rationale for Excluding or Confirming Diagnoses:**
  - Pyelonephritis and hydronephrosis: Initially confirmed but did not explain persistent symptoms after treatment.
  - Renal malignancy: Initially masked by inflammatory and infectious features on imaging, and only diagnosed definitively postoperatively.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: High-grade invasive renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation, complicated by hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: High-grade invasive renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Initial Presentation Analysis:** The patient initially presented with right waist distension and hydronephrosis due to a ureteral stone. This was treated with percutaneous renal puncture and ureteroscopic lithotripsy.  This indicates a history of kidney stones and potential for urinary tract obstruction, but at that time, no tumors were identified.

<step 2> **Recurrent Symptoms and Progression:** One year later, the patient re-presents with right waist pain, intermittent fever, and imaging showing an enlarged kidney with hydronephrosis and internal separation. This is a significant change from the initial presentation. The fever suggests infection or inflammation, while the internal separation and kidney enlargement are concerning and point towards a more complex pathology than simple stone recurrence.

<step 3> **Failure of Conservative Treatments:** Empirical antibiotics and drainage failed to resolve the symptoms. This is a critical point.  Typical pyelonephritis or obstruction due to stones should respond to antibiotics and drainage, respectively. The failure of these treatments strongly suggests a non-infectious or treatment-resistant underlying cause.

<step 4> **Nephrectomy and Pathology - Definitive Diagnosis:**  Due to the non-resolving symptoms and non-functioning kidney, a nephrectomy was performed. The post-nephrectomy pathology report is the definitive diagnostic tool in this case. It reveals "high-grade invasive renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation." This is a specific and aggressive type of cancer originating in the renal pelvis.

<step 5> **Integration of Findings:**  The clinical history of hydronephrosis, evolving pain and fever, imaging findings of kidney enlargement and internal separation, failure of standard treatments, and finally, the pathology report are all consistent with a diagnosis of renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma. The initial stone history might have masked or delayed the diagnosis of the underlying malignancy, or perhaps the initial hydronephrosis was secondary to an early, undetected tumor causing obstruction.  The squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation indicates a more aggressive and less common variant of urothelial carcinoma, known for its poorer prognosis.

### Answer:
Renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation

reasoning_eval.step: **Initial Presentation Analysis:** The patient initially presented with right waist distension and hydronephrosis due to a ureteral stone. This was treated with percutaneous renal puncture and ureteroscopic lithotripsy.  This indicates a history of kidney stones and potential for urinary tract obstruction, but at that time, no tumors were identified.

**Recurrent Symptoms and Progression:** One year later, the patient re-presents with right waist pain, intermittent fever, and imaging showing an enlarged kidney with hydronephrosis and internal separation. This is a significant change from the initial presentation. The fever suggests infection or inflammation, while the internal separation and kidney enlargement are concerning and point towards a more complex pathology than simple stone recurrence.

**Failure of Conservative Treatments:** Empirical antibiotics and drainage failed to resolve the symptoms. This is a critical point.  Typical pyelonephritis or obstruction due to stones should respond to antibiotics and drainage, respectively. The failure of these treatments strongly suggests a non-infectious or treatment-resistant underlying cause.

**Nephrectomy and Pathology - Definitive Diagnosis:**  Due to the non-resolving symptoms and non-functioning kidney, a nephrectomy was performed. The post-nephrectomy pathology report is the definitive diagnostic tool in this case. It reveals "high-grade invasive renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation." This is a specific and aggressive type of cancer originating in the renal pelvis.

**Integration of Findings:**  The clinical history of hydronephrosis, evolving pain and fever, imaging findings of kidney enlargement and internal separation, failure of standard treatments, and finally, the pathology report are all consistent with a diagnosis of renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma. The initial stone history might have masked or delayed the diagnosis of the underlying malignancy, or perhaps the initial hydronephrosis was secondary to an early, undetected tumor causing obstruction.  The squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation indicates a more aggressive and less common variant of urothelial carcinoma, known for its poorer prognosis.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial Presentation (Severe Hydronephrosis, Likely Infectious Origin)

Acute obstructive pyelonephritis and hydronephrosis secondary to ureteral stone were considered; percutaneous drainage, antimicrobial treatment, and later stone removal were performed. Renal malignancy was not initially suspected as imaging and ureteroscopy showed no evidence of tumors.

Hydronephrosis and persistent fevers despite aggressive antimicrobial therapy raised suspicion of intractable pyelonephritis or atypical pathology. Recurrent infection from abnormal renal anatomy vs. an underlying malignancy were considered. Imaging features and low kidney function justified right nephrectomy without further advanced diagnostics.

Postoperative pathological evaluation confirmed high-grade invasive renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with unusual squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation. Immunohistochemical markers supported the malignant epithelial and mesenchymal origin.
Step 4: Rationale for Excluding or Confirming Diagnoses

Pyelonephritis and hydronephrosis were initially confirmed but did not explain persistent symptoms after treatment. Renal malignancy was initially masked by inflammatory and infectious features on imaging and only diagnosed definitively postoperatively.
High-grade invasive renal pelvic urothelial carcinoma with squamous and sarcomatoid differentiation, complicated by hydronephrosis and pyelonephritis.
